entities.description: A near-diplomatic digital transcription pairing the Sloane 3486 and Hunter 307 versions line by line for comparison.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME comparative transcription CME301
notes: M. Teresa Tavormina transcribed the Sloane stream; Paul F. Schaffner transcribed the Hunter stream and the revision history attributes transcription and markup work to pfs.
Each of the sixty P elements contains one Sloane unit followed by one LB and one Hunter unit. Those P/LB pairs are editorial alignment units, not manuscript line counts.

rights.basis: The exact U-M header states that the materials are in the public domain and that neither the university nor the editors claims copyright in them.
rights.component: Modern comparative transcription and markup represented by CME301

entities.description: The opening passage of the Middle English On Bloodletting tradition explaining how a physician should inspect blood for thickness, temperature, smell, wateriness, surface appearance, color, foam, and other diagnostic signs.
entities.label: Middle English "Three Inspections" hematoscopy passage
notes: Guy de Chauliac is responsible for the underlying Latin surgical material; the Middle English translator or translators are anonymous.
The evidence does not yet establish whether Sloane, Hunter, and Royal descend from one Middle English translation or from more than one translation or recension.

rights.basis: The text is preserved in fifteenth-century manuscripts, and the U-M source header expressly calls the represented materials public domain.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English wording
notes: This does not determine rights in modern transcriptions, editions, photographs, catalog descriptions, or provider interfaces.

entities.description: The bloodletting material attributed to Guy de Chauliac within the Chirurgia magna, the source work behind the Middle English versions.
entities.label: Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting in the Chirurgia magna
notes: This abstract source-work entity does not identify the Middle English translator's physical Latin exemplar.

rights.basis: The Chirurgia magna was composed in 1363 by an author who died in 1368.
rights.component: Underlying fourteenth-century source work

access.accessMethod: Email Archives & Special Collections with shelfmark MS Hunter 307 (U.7.1) and the requested physical surfaces
access.entityLabel: University of Glasgow Library, MS Hunter 307 (U.7.1)
notes: The enquiry service may share up to twenty non-publication-standard quick snaps for targeted research, but that does not promise a whole-codex copy.

A complete-source request should separately specify covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and every surviving manuscript leaf, then identify CME301's work portion as f. 165v line 14 through f. 166r line 25.
access.provider: University of Glasgow Library, Archives & Special Collections

dateStatements.label: MED stencil date preserved in CME

holding.notes: The University of Glasgow's current digitised-collections guide identifies MS Hunter 307 and routes users to the University of Málaga WebHunter project.

entities.label: University of Glasgow Library, MS Hunter 307 (U.7.1)
notes: The On Bloodletting text begins at f. 165v line 14 and continues through f. 166v.
CME301 stops at f. 166r line 25, immediately before the sentence beginning 'For garsyng and ventosyng' at line 26.
The 2023 dialect study localizes the language of ff. 165v-166v to the Midlands, probably Huntingdonshire; that is a witness-level scribal-language claim, not a work-wide translation origin.

physicalDescription.dimensions: Binding 200 x 140 mm; leaves 190 x 130 mm; written space 147-150 x 90-95 mm for the target
physicalDescription.extent: 167 surviving folios reported by WebHunter; the target treatise occupies ff. 165v-166v
notes: The target is written in one column of twenty-six lines per folio in black ink, with alternating red and blue paragraph marks and decorated initials.

The 2023 study describes Gothic textura features in the target leaves; WebHunter's broader codex description uses Bastard Anglicana/Anglicana Formata terminology. These source-scoped descriptions are retained rather than forced into a single label.
The manuscript belonged to William Hunter, who bequeathed it to the University of Glasgow; the university has held his legacy since 1807.

entities.label: British Library, Royal MS 18 A VI
notes: The exact current catalog component reads: Guy de Chauliac on hematoscopy, scarifying and cupping, translated to Middle English, beginning 'A phisycian behoveth to knowe in manere inspeccions'.

The independently cataloged, closely corresponding incipit and matching content attribution make Royal 18 A VI a secure collateral witness, but CME301 does not transcribe it and the Royal subsection's exact end remains unverified.
No Digitised Content is exposed by the current BL record as of 2026-07-14.

physicalDescription.dimensions: 210 x 140 mm, with smaller later sections

No local copy is recorded because WebHunter's specific Copyright page prohibits copying and image use without prior written consent, while the footer's broad CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 assertion does not clearly identify whether the manuscript JPEGs are included. localCopies.label: Complete 2023 article and Hunter 307 semi-diplomatic edition
notes: Complete relative to the 24-page article, not to the physical manuscript.
The PDF includes full-page images of Hunter 307 ff. 165v, 166r, and 166v by permission of Glasgow University Library; those three plates remain only partial manuscript-image coverage.
The exact current provider download has the recorded SHA-256 and byte count. Poppler pdfinfo 26.07.0 reports 24 readable pages and pdftotext 26.07.0 extracted all pages.
qpdf 12.3.2 --check completed successfully with recoverable linearization hint-table, shared-object, and page-offset warnings. The provider file is retained byte-for-byte without repair.

workPortion.label: CME301-equivalent Hunter passage and its manuscript plates within the complete article
locators: PDF pp. 8-9 / journal pp. 114-115: f. 165v plate and transcription; CME301 begins at manuscript line 14
PDF pp. 10-11 / journal pp. 116-117: f. 166r plate and transcription; CME301 ends at manuscript line 25
notes: PDF pp. 12-13 / journal pp. 118-119 reproduce and transcribe f. 166v, which belongs to the scarifying and cupping continuation outside CME301.
The complete article also retains the study, apparatus, bibliography, and other matter outside the corpus selection.

bibliographic.citation: Laura Esteban-Segura and Carlos Soriano-Jiménez, 'Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting in Glasgow, University Library, MS Hunter 307 (ff. 165v-166v),' Revista Canaria de Estudios Ingleses 87 (2023): 107-130, https://doi.org/10.25145/j.recaesin.2023.87.06.
bibliographic.title: Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting in Glasgow, University Library, MS Hunter 307 (ff. 165v-166v)

entities.description: A later semi-diplomatic edition and study of the complete Hunter On Bloodletting text at ff. 165v-166v, including full-page images of the three manuscript sides, codicological and palaeographical analysis, and dialect localization.
entities.label: Esteban-Segura and Soriano-Jiménez's 2023 Hunter 307 edition
notes: This article postdates the May 2020 CME transcription and is supporting scholarship, not an ancestor of CME301.
Its edition makes the CME boundary auditable: CME301 ends after the Hunter reading 'quesy and viscouse humours' at f. 166r line 25, while the article continues at line 26 and through f. 166v.

rights.basis: The journal landing page applies Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International.

next_steps: Obtain or inspect Royal 18 A VI ff. 54r-55v and Sloane 3486 f. 147v images.
Collate all three complete components against the relevant Latin passage and consult later transmission scholarship.
openQuestions.question: Do the Sloane 3486, Hunter 307, and Royal 18 A VI versions descend from one Middle English translation or from distinct translations or recensions?

next_steps: Restore or obtain access to the eVK2 occurrence search for VK 0659.00 and audit related IMEP records.
Continue exact-incipit searches in current institutional catalogs and compare any candidate's opening and extent.
notes: The 2023 article reported that complete witness tracking remained uncertain; the current Royal catalog record supplies one additional witness with a closely corresponding incipit not discussed there.
openQuestions.question: Are further Middle English witnesses of this exact On Bloodletting passage known beyond the three currently verified copies?

next_steps: Request a reference image of ff. 54r-55v from the British Library and align the text with CME301.
Record precise line or folio-side boundaries if the comparison confirms them.
openQuestions.question: Where exactly within Royal MS 18 A VI ff. 54r-55v does the CME301-equivalent Three Inspections section end?

next_steps: Ask the WebHunter project or University of Málaga to identify in writing which components the footer license covers and whether the specific Copyright-page restrictions remain operative.
For manuscript images, also confirm reuse terms with Glasgow Archives & Special Collections and any identified rightsholders.
Until clarified, do not cache, redistribute, or publish the WebHunter transcription or JPEGs in reliance on the footer license.
openQuestions.question: Does WebHunter's footer-level CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 statement apply to the exact Hunter 307 transcription and manuscript JPEGs despite the more specific Copyright page's permission requirements?

next_steps: Ask Glasgow Archives & Special Collections or the WebHunter project for a canonical physical-object inventory.
Do not label the portal a complete physical-object facsimile until that inventory is reconciled with the part-based page sequences.
openQuestions.question: Do the three overlapping WebHunter part sequences collectively photograph every physical surface of Hunter 307, including covers, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves, blanks, and edges?

next_steps: Request written terms separately for private research storage and for public or commercial repository reuse.
If permission is granted, preserve the response and build one audited, gitignored full-source image bundle with the CME301 folio mapping.
openQuestions.question: Will Glasgow University Library and the WebHunter rightsholders authorize a private complete image cache or public repository reuse of Hunter 307 images?

next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services whether legacy digitization, microfilm, or photography exists for either shelfmark.
If no reusable complete surrogate exists, obtain quotes specifying the whole physical object separately from the target folios.
openQuestions.question: Can complete public facsimiles or pre-existing institutional reproductions of Sloane MS 3486 or Royal MS 18 A VI be identified?

subject.description: Transmission discussion, edition opening, and boundary evidence

reviewNotes: CME301 is one comparative digital transcription of a coherent work-part represented by two witness streams, not sixty works and not two independently cataloged corpus works.
The Royal MS 18 A VI witness is collateral evidence for the medieval transmission and is not represented as an upstream source of the University of Michigan encoding.

Public access, local caching, copyright, and permission to publish are recorded separately. WebHunter's technically reachable JPEGs were not bulk-cached because its specific Copyright page prohibits copying without permission and the scope of the site's conflicting footer CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license is unresolved.

The sibling wrapper at CME/source/CME_additions/CME301.xml was not modeled as a separate publication ancestor because the repository publication colophon selects CME/source/recent_changes/additions_2023/CME301.xml and the two files share the represented body while differing at the wrapper/header layer.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the publication-selected University of Michigan CME XML for a May 2020 near-diplomatic comparative transcription of the opening hematoscopy passage of Guy de Chauliac's On Bloodletting, published with a full header in 2023. Its sixty prose alignment units pair a transcription of British Library Sloane MS 3486, f. 147v, with one of Glasgow University Library MS Hunter 307 (U.7.1), f. 165v line 14 through f. 166r line 25. Hunter 307 continues with scarifying and cupping after the CME301 boundary. A current British Library component record establishes Royal MS 18 A VI, ff. 54r-55v, as a third collateral Middle English witness beginning with a closely corresponding incipit. WebHunter provides public part-based images and a separate transcription of Hunter 307. Its footer offers a broad CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license while its specific Copyright page reserves the images, transcriptions, and site information and requires permission; because the scope conflict is unresolved, no manuscript-image bundle was cached. The complete open 2023 scholarly article and Hunter edition is cached locally, and its three embedded Hunter manuscript plates are modeled separately as a partial facsimile.

catalogingSubject.label: "Three Inspections" Hematoscopy
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The coherent opening hematoscopy passage of the anonymous Middle English On Bloodletting tradition represented in CME301 by parallel Sloane 3486 and Hunter 307 versions. It is a work-part, not the complete On Bloodletting text, either complete manuscript, the sixty XML alignment units, or the modern digital encoding.

contentParts.summary: A continuous prose instruction for interpreting the visible and sensory qualities of blood drawn by phlebotomy as diagnostic signs of humoral and bodily conditions.

dateStatements.display: The Latin Chirurgia magna containing Guy de Chauliac's bloodletting material was composed in 1363

scope.description: Underlying Latin source work, not the Middle English translation date

assertion.notes: The broad normalized terminus avoids assigning the translation a false single year or assuming that all surviving copies reflect one translation event.

dateStatements.display: Translated into Middle English no later than the early fifteenth-century Hunter 307 attestation; exact date unknown

assertion.notes: Hunter's 1400-1449 range overlaps Sloane's 1425-1474 range, while the Royal component is dated only to 1400-1499. The 1400-1449 normalization records the securely supported early-fifteenth-century attestation window without selecting one witness as definitively earliest or excluding a lost earlier exemplar.

dateStatements.display: At least one attestation, Hunter 307, is early fifteenth century, but its production range overlaps Sloane 3486 and Royal 18 A VI is only broadly dated; no single earliest witness is established

extent.description: One coherent prose hematoscopy passage. The repository representation contains sixty paired P/LB alignment units, each with a Sloane and Hunter reading; those sixty pairs are not independent works or manuscript line counts.

formStatements.description: Continuous instructional and diagnostic medical prose.

assertion.notes: The cataloging subject is the hematoscopy opening of the longer phlebotomy text, not its scarifying and cupping continuation.
Bloodletting is treated as a surgical practice within Guy de Chauliac's Chirurgia magna.
The current BL Royal component record explicitly calls the text a Middle English translation; the precise relation among the three witness versions remains open.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English titles, editorial notes, and article prose belong to representation or research layers and do not make the medieval cataloging subject bilingual.
The title is modern and supplied; no manuscript title for this shared passage has been established.
M. Teresa Tavormina and Paul F. Schaffner are recorded as transcribers of the CME representation, not as medieval authors.

The source header's modern keyword 'Hematology' is retained in lineage evidence but not treated as proof that the medieval passage belongs to modern scientific hematology; hematoscopy, phlebotomy, humoral medicine, and diagnosis are the more precise discovery concepts.
Royal MS 18 A VI is a newly verified collateral witness from a current institutional catalog and must not be presented as a manuscript source used to create CME301.

next_steps: Obtain complete target images or transcripts for all three witnesses.
Collate their full hematoscopy passages and the relevant Latin source before assigning a single translation identity.

next_steps: Keep witness dates and dialect localizations separate while comparing the three versions.
Seek scholarship or textual evidence that distinguishes translation language from later scribal copying.
notes: Hunter's probable Huntingdonshire localization is evidence about that copy's language, not a secure work-level place of translation.

next_steps: Request or inspect Royal MS 18 A VI ff. 54r-55v.

subjects.label: Hematoscopy and inspection of drawn blood

assertion.notes: The passage explains what observable blood properties signify about bodily corruption, humoral predominance, moisture, dryness, liver coldness, wind, and related conditions.

summaries.value: The passage instructs a physician to inspect blood during and after bloodletting. Thickness, thinness, heat, cold, congealing, harshness, fat, smell, wateriness, foam, surface evenness, color, brittleness, toughness, and bubbles are interpreted as signs of bodily moisture or dryness, corruption, leprosy, liver coldness, wind, burning, mortification, and the predominance of choler, phlegm, melancholy, or other humours.

CME301 compares the shared opening passage in Sloane MS 3486, f. 147v, and Hunter 307, f. 165v line 14 through f. 166r line 25. It excludes Hunter's continuation on scarifying and cupping beginning at f. 166r line 26 and continuing through f. 166v. Royal MS 18 A VI, ff. 54r-55v, is a third collateral witness to the longer hematoscopy, scarifying, and cupping text; its exact internal end point for the CME301-equivalent passage has not yet been verified.

The XML's sixty P elements each align one Sloane unit with one Hunter unit across a single LB. They are comparative editorial units, not sixty numbered intellectual items, two separate works, or trustworthy counts of manuscript lines. The cataloged unit is one prose work-part with two represented witness versions.

assertion.notes: An editorial discovery title in the source header and text head, not a manuscript title.
A project-supplied contextual title for discovery; the attribution is to the underlying source work, while the Middle English translator remains anonymous.
